categories.name: AlexKontorovich/PrimeNumberTheoremAnd
categories.reasoning: This project aims to formalize the Prime Number Theorem and related results like the Prime Number Theorem in Arithmetic Progressions and potentially the Chebotarev density theorem. It is a focused effort to formalize specific mathematical theorems rather than create reusable libraries or teaching materials.

github_metadata.description: blueprint for prime number theorem and more

categories.name: FormalizedFormalLogic/Foundation
categories.reasoning: This repository is dedicated to formalizing mathematical logic in Lean 4, including specific results like completeness theorems for propositional logic and Kripke semantics. It follows the pattern of a focused formalization project with a book documenting the formalized theorems and theories.

github_metadata.description: Formalization of Mathematical Logic

categories.name: HEPLean/PhysLean
categories.reasoning: PhysLean is a project focused on formalizing and digitalizing specific results from physics into Lean 4. Like mathematics formalization projects (FLT, Carleson), it aims to create formal proofs of domain-specific theorems and results, but in the physics domain rather than pure mathematics.

github_metadata.description: A project to digitalise results from physics into Lean.

categories.name: ImperialCollegeLondon/FLT
categories.reasoning: This repository is specifically dedicated to formalizing the proof of Fermat's Last Theorem in Lean. It's a major mathematical theorem formalization project, which is the defining characteristic of the formalization category.

github_metadata.description: Ongoing Lean formalisation of the proof of Fermat's Last Theorem

categories.name: PatrickMassot/GlimpseOfLean
categories.reasoning: This is explicitly an introductory tutorial for learning theorem proving in Lean, designed for people to get started in 2-3 hours. It contains structured learning tracks (Introduction.lean, Shorter.lean) aimed at teaching newcomers how to write proofs in Lean.

github_metadata.description: An introduction to theorem proving in Lean for the impatient.

categories.name: Verified-zkEVM/ZKLib
categories.reasoning: This is a reusable library providing a modular and composable framework for formally verifying SNARKs and zero-knowledge proofs. It is designed to be used by other projects (specifically the verified-zkevm effort) rather than formalizing a specific theorem or serving as a teaching resource.

github_metadata.description: Formally Verified Arguments of Knowledge in Lean

categories.name: YaelDillies/LeanCamCombi
categories.reasoning: This repository formalizes specific university courses (Cambridge Part II and Part III) in graph theory and combinatorics. While it contains mathematical content, its primary purpose is to support teaching and learning of these specific academic courses.

github_metadata.description: Formalisation of the Cambridge Part II and Part III courses Graph Theory, Combinatorics, Extremal and Probabilistic Combinatorics in Lean

categories.name: alexkeizer/QpfTypes
categories.reasoning: This is a WIP package that implements a `codata` command for defining coinductive types in Lean 4, providing new language-level functionality. It's a development tool that extends Lean's definitional capabilities for datatypes and codatatypes.

github_metadata.description: A WIP definitional (co)datatype package for Lean4

categories.name: cmu-l3/llmlean
categories.reasoning: This repository provides tooling that integrates LLMs with Lean for tactic suggestions and proof completion. It's a development aid that enhances the Lean proof assistant experience rather than formalizing specific mathematical content or serving as a library.

github_metadata.description: LLMs + Lean, on your laptop or in the cloud

categories.name: dwrensha/compfiles
categories.reasoning: This repository is a catalog of olympiad-style math competition problems formalized in Lean 4, with a dashboard tracking their completion status. It serves as a benchmark collection of formalized math problems similar to IMO and miniF2F datasets.

github_metadata.description: Catalog Of Math Problems Formalized In Lean

categories.name: emilyriehl/infinity-cosmos
categories.reasoning: This project is focused on formalizing infinity-cosmos theory, which is a specific mathematical theory in higher category theory. The repository includes a blueprint for the formalization, indicating it's a structured effort to formalize a particular mathematical domain rather than creating general-purpose tools or teaching materials.

github_metadata.description: A blueprint for a formalization of infinity-cosmos theory in Lean.

categories.name: eric-wieser/lean-matrix-cookbook
categories.reasoning: This project is formalizing a specific mathematical reference work (The Matrix Cookbook) by proving its lemmas in Lean. It's focused on systematically translating and verifying the mathematical content of that particular document rather than creating a general-purpose library or teaching resource.

github_metadata.description: The matrix cookbook, proved in the Lean theorem prover

categories.name: fpvandoorn/carleson
categories.reasoning: This repository is dedicated to formalizing a specific mathematical theorem (Carleson's theorem) in Lean, with a complete blueprint and structured proof project. It fits the formalization category as it focuses on proving a particular mathematical result rather than creating reusable libraries or teaching materials.

github_metadata.description: A formalized proof of Carleson's theorem in Lean

categories.name: frenzymath/jixia
categories.reasoning: This is a static analysis tool specifically designed for Lean 4, used for building IDE capabilities and extracting data for machine learning. It's a development tool that aids in working with Lean code rather than formalizing mathematics or teaching.

github_metadata.description: A static analysis tool for Lean 4.

categories.name: lean-ja/lean-by-example
categories.reasoning: The repository name 'lean-by-example' and description 'コード例で学ぶ Lean 言語' (Learn Lean language through code examples) clearly indicate this is a teaching resource. It uses mdbook format to provide learning materials and reference examples for Lean 4.

github_metadata.description: コード例で学ぶ Lean 言語

categories.name: leanprover-community/NNG4
categories.reasoning: The Natural Number Game is an interactive tutorial designed to teach Lean through game-like proof exercises about natural numbers. It serves as a learning resource for introducing users to theorem proving in Lean.

github_metadata.description: Natural Number Game

categories.name: leanprover-community/aesop
categories.reasoning: Aesop is a proof search tactic and automation tool for Lean 4, similar to Isabelle's `auto`. It provides white-box automation capabilities that extend Lean's built-in proof search functionality, making it a development tool for Lean users rather than a library, formalization, or teaching resource.

github_metadata.description: White-box automation for Lean 4

categories.name: leanprover-community/batteries
categories.reasoning: Batteries is explicitly described as an extended library providing reusable data structures and tactics for both computer-science and mathematics applications in Lean 4. It serves as a foundational collection of utilities intended to be imported and used by other projects.

github_metadata.description: The "batteries included" extended library for the Lean programming language and theorem prover

categories.name: leanprover-community/duper
categories.reasoning: Duper is an automatic proof-producing theorem prover for Lean 4, similar to Isabelle's Metis. It's a tool that aids in Lean development by automatically finding proofs, rather than being a library of mathematical content or a formalization project.

categories.name: leanprover-community/iris-lean
categories.reasoning: This is a port of Iris, a well-established concurrent separation logic framework, intended to provide reusable infrastructure for reasoning about concurrent programs and logical relations. Like the original Coq/Rocq version, it's designed as a foundational library that other projects can build upon rather than formalizing a specific theorem or serving as a teaching tool.

github_metadata.description: Lean 4 port of Iris, a higher-order concurrent separation logic framework

categories.name: leanprover-community/mathlib4
categories.reasoning: mathlib4 is explicitly described as 'The math library of Lean 4' and serves as the standard, reusable mathematical library for the Lean theorem prover ecosystem. It provides foundational mathematical definitions, theorems, and structures that other Lean projects depend upon.

github_metadata.description: The math library of Lean 4

categories.name: leanprover-community/quote4
categories.reasoning: quote4 is a Lean 4 metaprogramming tool that provides type-safe expression quotations for constructing Lean expressions in meta-level code. It's a development utility that helps Lean 4 programmers write metaprograms more conveniently, rather than being a library of mathematical content or a formalization project.

github_metadata.description: Intuitive, type-safe expression quotations for Lean 4.

categories.name: leanprover-community/sphere-eversion
categories.reasoning: This project formalizes a specific mathematical theorem about sphere eversions, with a completed proof of the main theorem. It focuses on formalizing a particular mathematical result rather than creating reusable libraries or teaching materials.

github_metadata.description: Formalization of the existence of sphere eversions

categories.name: leanprover/verso
categories.reasoning: Verso is a documentation authoring tool specifically designed for Lean. It's a development aid that helps create various forms of documentation and instructional materials for Lean projects, making it clearly a Lean-specific utility tool.

github_metadata.description: Lean documentation authoring tool

categories.name: lecopivo/SciLean
categories.reasoning: SciLean is a reusable library for scientific computing tasks like solving differential equations, optimization, and machine learning in Lean 4. It provides general-purpose functionality intended to be used across multiple projects, similar to how mathlib provides mathematical foundations.

github_metadata.description: Scientific computing in Lean 4

categories.name: mo271/FormalBook
categories.reasoning: This project is specifically dedicated to formalizing the proofs from a well-known mathematical book 'Proofs from THE BOOK'. It focuses on translating specific, established mathematical proofs into Lean4, which is the hallmark of a formalization project rather than creating reusable libraries or teaching materials.

categories.name: nomeata/loogle
categories.reasoning: Loogle is a search tool specifically designed for Lean/Mathlib, providing functionality through web interfaces, APIs, editor extensions, and command-line access. It's a development utility that helps users find declarations and theorems in Mathlib rather than being a library or formalization itself.

github_metadata.description: Mathlib search tool

categories.name: teorth/pfr
categories.reasoning: This repository is dedicated to formalizing a specific mathematical result - the Polynomial Freiman-Ruzsa conjecture and its proof. It focuses on proving a particular theorem rather than building reusable libraries or teaching materials.

github_metadata.description: Repository for formalization of the Polynomial Freiman Ruzsa conjecture (and related results)

categories.name: ufmg-smite/lean-smt
categories.reasoning: This repository provides Lean tactics that integrate SMT solvers into the Lean proof assistant workflow. It's a development tool that extends Lean's capabilities by interfacing with external automated reasoning systems.

github_metadata.description: Tactics for discharging Lean goals into SMT solvers.

categories.name: yangky11/miniF2F-lean4
categories.reasoning: This is a Lean 4 port of MiniF2F, which is explicitly described as 'a cross-system benchmark for formal Olympiad-level mathematics'. The repository contains formalized competition-level math problems used for evaluating automated theorem proving systems.
